nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods; USDA FoodData Central — Shrimp, cooked (FDC ID 175180) and Mango, raw (FDC ID 169910)
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India; U.S. Department of Agriculture
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Eraal Maangai Masala as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN prawn curry and mango data combined with USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked shrimp (FDC ID 175180), raw mango (FDC ID 169910), and coconut milk (FDC ID 169904). The input record values (energy 137.14 kcal, protein 8.57 g, fat 8.57 g, carbohydrate 7.14 g per 100 g) are consistent with a prawn-coconut milk curry with mango and are retained as the best available estimate. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water are set to -1 as detailed composite values are not available in approved sources for this specific preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for prawn curry with vegetables
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Eraal Maangai Masala. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on analogous prawn/seafood curry with vegetables from the University of Sydney GI Database. The low GI is supported by: (1) low carbohydrate content (7.14 g/100 g), (2) high protein from prawns slowing gastric emptying, (3) fat from coconut milk further attenuating glucose response, and (4) raw mango contributing modest natural sugars with fibre. Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 24.99) / 100 ≈ 11.2. Reported as 11.2 per 350 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 271.43 mg/100 g; borderline sodium load with high cholesterol from prawns
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200 g and avoid adding extra salt during cooking, as the 271 mg/100 g sodium content approaches the threshold for a high-sodium dish; using light coconut milk and increasing potassium-rich vegetables (tomatoes, onions) can partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (7.14 g/100 g), high protein (8.57 g/100 g), low GI (~45)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Eraal Maangai Masala is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderate portions (150–200 g) paired with a low-GI carbohydrate such as brown rice or chapati, as the low carbohydrate content, high prawn protein, and spice base (turmeric, cumin) collectively support glycaemic control and reduce post-prandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 5.14 g/100 g from coconut milk; cholesterol 71.43 mg/100 g from prawns
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish in moderation (≤150 g per occasion) due to the saturated fat contribution from coconut milk (5.14 g/100 g) and dietary cholesterol from prawns (71.43 mg/100 g); substituting light coconut milk and increasing the proportion of vegetables can reduce the saturated fat density while preserving the omega-3 and lean protein benefits of prawns.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 5.14 g/100 g from coconut milk; nightshade vegetables (tomatoes, green chillies)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should use light coconut milk to reduce saturated fat below 3 g/100 g and monitor tolerance to nightshade vegetables (tomatoes, green chillies), while retaining turmeric and ginger for their evidence-based anti-inflammatory polyphenol content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Eraal Maangai Masala (Tamil: இறால் மாங்காய் மசாலா) is a South Indian prawn and raw mango curry popular in Singapore's Indian-cuisine restaurants and hawker stalls. The dish features fresh prawns (eraal) simmered in a rich, aromatic coconut milk-based gravy infused with the tartness of raw green mango (maangai) and a traditional spice blend of turmeric, cumin, coriander, mustard seeds, curry leaves, green chillies, ginger, and garlic. The cooking method involves tempering whole spices in oil, building a tomato-onion masala base, adding raw mango pieces for sourness, incorporating coconut milk for creaminess, and finishing with prawns simmered until just cooked. The result is a vibrant orange-red curry with a balance of tangy, spicy, and mildly sweet flavours, with a tender-firm prawn texture and a silky, medium-thick gravy. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 137 kcal, 8.6 g protein, 8.6 g fat (5.1 g saturated from coconut milk), and 7.1 g carbohydrates with 1.4 g dietary fibre. Sodium is 271 mg/100 g, placing it in the borderline range for sodium-sensitive individuals. Cholesterol is 71 mg/100 g from prawns. The dish is halal and gluten-free. Clinically, the low carbohydrate content and high protein make it suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management when paired with low-GI staples; the saturated fat from coconut milk warrants moderation for cardiovascular and rheumatoid arthritis patients. The spice base provides anti-inflammatory polyphenols (curcumin from turmeric, gingerols from ginger). A standard serving is approximately 350 g (one bowl), providing roughly 480 kcal.

The input record values are internally consistent with the expected macronutrient profile of a prawn-coconut milk curry and are retained as the best available estimate. Confidence is medium because the values are derived from component-level adaptation rather than a direct measurement of this specific dish.
